name: Maderuelo and Campo de San Pedro Loop
name_original: Embalse de Linares del Arroyo y Estación de Campo de San Pedro — circular desde Mareduelo
description: Cycle a moderate 15.6-mile loop from Maderuelo, exploring Linares Reservoir, Campo de San Pedro Station, and historic villages.
region_intro: Touring cycling around Maderuelo features routes through the region's varied terrain, characterized by rolling hills, river valleys, and agricultural landscapes. The area offers a mix of open country roads and paths that connect historic villages. Elevations are generally moderate, providing accessible cycling experiences. The landscape provides opportunities for both leisurely rides and more challenging routes. Best touring cycling routes around Maderuelo The most popular touring cycling route is Maderuelo and Campo de San Pedro Loop, a 15.6 miles (25.1 km) trail that takes 1 hour 35 minutes to complete. This moderate route passes through rural landscapes and charming villages. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Vadocondes Historic Center – Church of San Andrés, Zazuar loop from Vadocondes, an easy 12.6 miles (20.3 km) path. This route explores historic centers and river plains. Local touring cyclists also love the Riaza River Gorge – Valdevacas de Montejo loop from Montejo de la Vega de la Serrezuela, a 29.3 miles (47.1 km) trail leading through the Riaza River Gorge, often completed in about 4 hours 24 minutes. Touring cycling around Maderuelo is defined by river gorges, rolling hills, and historic villages. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy rides to more difficult routes. The routes in Maderuelo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from a few reviews. More than 40 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Maderuelo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Maderuelo?
A: There are over 15 touring cycling routes documented around Maderuelo on komoot, offering a variety of experiences for different skill levels.

Q: What are the typical distances and difficulties of touring cycling routes in Maderuelo?
A: Routes vary significantly. You'll find &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31887508" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;easy paths&lt;/a&gt; around 12 miles (20 km) exploring historic centers, while more challenging routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38151978" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Campo de San Pedro Station – Plaza Mayor de Riaza loop&lt;/a&gt; can extend to over 50 miles (80 km) with significant elevation changes.

Q: Are there family-friendly touring cycling routes in Maderuelo?
A: Yes, there are routes suitable for families. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31887508" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vadocondes Historic Center – Church of San Andrés, Zazuar loop from Vadocondes&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 12.6-mile (20.3 km) path that explores historic centers and river plains, making it a good option for a leisurely family ride.

Q: Can I bring my dog on touring cycling routes in Maderuelo?
A: While many outdoor areas in Spain are dog-friendly, it's always best to check local regulations for specific trails or protected natural areas. Generally, dogs on a leash are welcome on public paths, but ensure they are comfortable with cycling and that you carry water for them.

Q: Are there circular touring cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many routes in the Maderuelo area are designed as loops. For example, the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1111803971/maderuelo-and-campo-de-san-pedro-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Maderuelo and Campo de San Pedro Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a 15.6-mile (25.1 km) circular route that takes you through rural landscapes and charming villages.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Maderuelo?
A: Spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) generally offer the most pleasant conditions for touring cycling in Maderuelo. Temperatures are mild, and the landscapes are vibrant. Summers can be hot, especially in July and August, while winters can be chilly.

Q: Where can I park when cycling around Maderuelo?
A: Parking is typically available in the villages and towns that serve as starting points for routes, such as Maderuelo itself or nearby Montejo de la Vega de la Serrezuela. Look for designated public parking areas.

Q: Are there scenic viewpoints or natural attractions along the touring cycling routes?
A: Absolutely. The region is known for its natural beauty. You can find stunning views along routes that pass through the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5327014" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cliffs of the Hoces del Río Riaza&lt;/a&gt; or near the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2490089" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Linares Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38180644" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Riaza River Gorge loop&lt;/a&gt; is particularly scenic.

Q: What historical or cultural sites can I explore while touring cycling?
A: Maderuelo and its surroundings are rich in history. You can cycle through historic centers like Vadocondes, or pass by landmarks such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2544786" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Linares Reservoir Dam&lt;/a&gt;. The village of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6263013" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valdevacas de Montejo&lt;/a&gt; is also a notable settlement in the area.

Q: Are there places to stop for refreshments or food along the routes?
A: Many of the touring cycling routes connect or pass through small villages where you can find local cafes, bars, or restaurants. It's advisable to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially on longer or more remote sections, as services might be sparse between villages.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy the most about touring cycling in Maderuelo?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Reviewers often praise the diverse terrain, from river gorges to rolling hills, and the opportunity to explore historic villages and quiet country roads.

Q: Are there any less-known or 'hidden gem' touring cycling routes in the area?
A: While popular routes are well-loved, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40311027" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valdevacas de Montejo loop from Villaverde de Montejo&lt;/a&gt; offers a moderate 13.5-mile (21.7 km) ride through varied landscapes, providing a slightly less frequented but equally rewarding experience.
tips: The Linares reservoir is in a natural environment of special beauty and the Riaza gorges, less famous than those of the Duratón, can be traveled by MTB or on foot along the path that leaves from Montejo de la Vega de la Serrezuela to Maderuelo. Opposite Maderuelo, the hermitage of Vera Cruz is located at the foot of the reservoir, in an area with a beach and picnic tables, perfect for recharging your batteries.
San Pedro Field Station. Madrid - Burgos direct railway line (currently closed line).

faqs.question: What is the typical duration for cycling this route?
faqs.answer: The full circular route, approximately 25.1 km long, typically takes about 1 hour and 36 minutes to complete on a touring bicycle. There is also a shorter, easier variant of about 10 km, which is part of the 'Senda del Embalse de Linares del Arroyo' (PRC-SG 10).

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: The main 25.1 km route is considered moderate, requiring a good fitness level. However, a shorter, easier 10 km variant exists, which is part of the 'Senda del Embalse de Linares del Arroyo' (PRC-SG 10). The first kilometer of this shorter variant is accessible to people with reduced mobility and has a low difficulty.

faqs.question: Where can I park and start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route starts from the medieval village of Maderuelo. You can find parking in or around the village to begin your cycling tour.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for cycling on this route?
faqs.answer: The trail primarily consists of paved tracks, making it suitable for touring bikes. While most of the path is paved, some sections might be unpaved or present a challenge for certain bicycles.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the cycling route?
faqs.answer: Along the route, you can enjoy views of the Maderuelo and the Linares Reservoir, the medieval village of Maderuelo with its Romanesque churches like San Miguel and Santa María del Castillo, and the Hermitage of Vera Cruz. You'll also pass by the Campo de San Pedro Station. When water levels are low, the ruins of the old village of Linares del Arroyo, including the bell tower of San Juan Bautista church, can be seen in the reservoir. Panoramic views are exceptional from viewpoints like Mirador de La Rivilla.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route also passes through sections of the Embalse de Linares del Arroyo, Camino de la Puerta del Barrio, Bajada Alcacer, Calle Abajo, Camino Viejo del Chorrillo, Calle de las Eras, and Travesía Cerquilla.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Information regarding specific dog restrictions for this cycling route is not explicitly provided. However, generally, dogs are allowed on trails in natural areas, often requiring them to be on a leash. It's always best to check local regulations for the Hoces del Río Riaza Natural Park before you go.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for this route?
faqs.answer: No specific permits or entrance fees are mentioned for cycling this route. It passes through the Hoces del Río Riaza Natural Park, but general access for cycling is usually free.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The route offers beautiful scenery year-round. Spring and autumn generally provide pleasant temperatures and vibrant natural colors. In autumn, you might experience cooler weather, so dressing in layers is advisable.

faqs.question: Are there amenities like cafes or picnic spots along the way?
faqs.answer: The medieval village of Maderuelo, where the route starts, offers charming streets and likely some local establishments. Additionally, there are picnic areas by the Linares reservoir, providing excellent spots for rest and refreshments.

faqs.question: What kind of natural scenery can I expect?
faqs.answer: Cyclists can expect beautiful natural surroundings, including views of the Linares reservoir and hillsides adorned with oaks, holm oaks, and savin junipers. The route also passes through areas with abundant vegetation, orchards, cultivated lands, juniper forests, and pine forests, offering a diverse natural experience.
